frameworks.name: higher-order theory of consciousness
frameworks.one_line: subjective experience treated as a measurable cognitive-neuroscience target in psychopathology

frameworks.name: computational psychiatry
frameworks.one_line: framing of mental disorders as quantifiable disturbances of mental processes

frameworks.name: behaviorist symptom-marker model
frameworks.one_line: treats objective behavioral/physiological responses as primary marker, sidelining subjective report

core_question: Should the subjective experience of fear and anxiety, rather than objective behavioral and physiological responses, be the primary target in defining and treating mental disorders?
key_assumptions: subjective conscious experience is a distinct symptom dimension separable from behavioral/physiological response
mental disorders are defined by, and named for, their subjective mental qualities
cognitive neuroscience of consciousness has matured into a scientifically tractable approach
key_findings: behavioral and physiological symptoms of anxiety correlate weakly with subjective experience, contrary to common assumption
treatments developed using objective symptoms as marker of psychopathology have largely shown disappointing effectiveness
interpretations: sidelining mental qualities has plausibly contributed to limited efficacy of current anxiety-disorder treatments
consciousness-focused cognitive neuroscience offers a viable novel approach to understanding and treating mental disorders
